system_metadata.grounding_source: Value Proposition Design: How to Create Products Customers Want (2015)

spreadsheet_mirage.analogy: Building detailed spreadsheets that are completely made up and turn out to be wrong instead of field testing.
spreadsheet_mirage.action: Force quick, low-fidelity validations; forbid premature financial modeling.

cognitive_murder.analogy: Overwhelming stakeholders with too much abstract information at once during presentations.
cognitive_murder.action: Present information progressively, sticky note by sticky note, in visual formats.

business_killer_bomb.analogy: The lit fuse representing the most critical, unvalidated hypotheses that could blow up the entire business.
business_killer_bomb.action: Rank and test the most critical survival assumptions first before optimizing secondary elements.

earlyvangelist_ladder.analogy: Steve Blank's 5-stage earlyvangelist model: 1. Has problem -> 2. Aware of problem -> 3. Actively searching -> 4. Cobbled together solution -> 5. Has/can acquire budget.
earlyvangelist_ladder.action: Profile early adopters strictly based on their climb up this ladder.

pregnant_man_trap.analogy: The False Positive trap where testing data misleads you to see patterns that are not there (e.g. diagnosing a man as pregnant).
pregnant_man_trap.action: Test customer actions (willingness to invest/pay) rather than spoken words or opinions.

local_maximum_hill.analogy: Climbing a small hill and optimizing for a mediocre model when a much larger global maximum mountain exists next to it.
local_maximum_hill.action: Focus on learning and testing alternative business models before locking in and optimizing features.

buyer_of_value: How do they decide on a purchase?
What checkout, payment, or delivery obstacles do they encounter?
cocreator_of_value: How do customers provide reviews or design feedback?
How do they participate in product development?
transferrer_of_value: How do customers cancel subscriptions or dispose of products?
How do they resell, transfer, or recycle the offering?

high_value_job_rubric.important: Failing the job leads to extreme pains or misses out on essential gains.
high_value_job_rubric.tangible: Pains or gains related to the job are felt or experienced immediately or frequently.
high_value_job_rubric.unsatisfied: Current value propositions do not solve the job or leave massive unresolved pains.
high_value_job_rubric.lucrative: A large population has this job, or a small niche is willing to pay a heavy premium.

fit_check_rules.pain_relievers_must: Address extreme pains directly; do not create a generic list of features.
fit_check_rules.gain_creators_must: Deliver on essential, expected, or unexpected customer gains explicitly.
fit_check_rules.orphan_features: Any feature without a matching job, pain, or gain in the profile must be flagged as waste.

osterwalder_7_bm_questions.switching_costs: How easy or difficult is it for customers to switch to another company? (Locked in [10] vs Free to leave [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.recurring_revenues: Is every sale a new effort or does it result in quasi-guaranteed follow-up revenue? (Automatically recurring [10] vs Transactional [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.earning_versus_spending: Do you earn revenues before you incur costs or vice versa? (Earn before spend [10] vs Spend before earn [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.game_changing_cost_structure: Is your cost structure substantially better than your competitors? (50%+ cheaper [10] vs standard industry costs [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.others_do_the_work: How much does your business model get customers or partners to create value for free? (User-generated value [10] vs heavy internal labor [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.scalability: How easily can you grow your business model without running into resource bottlenecks? (Infinite/digital scalability [10] vs linear brick-and-mortar [0])
osterwalder_7_bm_questions.protection_from_competition: How strong are your business model moats compared to a standalone product? (Hard to copy/platform moats [10] vs easily copied [0])

interest_validation.name: Google AdWords Tracking
interest_validation.cta: Clicking an ad on a targeted keyword

interest_validation.name: Landing Page MVP
interest_validation.cta: Entering personal email for product updates

preference_validation.name: Speed Boat / Sail Boat Game
preference_validation.cta: Customers visually mapping pains as anchors and gains as sails

preference_validation.name: Product Box Customization
preference_validation.cta: Customers designing their dream product box and explaining feature prioritizations

preference_validation.name: Wizard of Oz Test
preference_validation.cta: Interacting with a manual backstage setup styled as a fully automated software

willingness_to_pay_validation.name: Presale / Crowdfunding campaign
willingness_to_pay_validation.cta: Submitting credit card for prepurchase or financial pledge

willingness_to_pay_validation.name: Mock Sales
willingness_to_pay_validation.cta: Initiating checkout with real payment intent (e.g. buy button click)

false_positive.name: False-Positive Trap (Pregnant Man Trap)
false_positive.risk: Seeing patterns that are not there (relying on polite spoken feedback or interview agreements).
false_positive.defense: Insist on experiments where customers must make real investments (providing emails, phone numbers, or credit cards) to prove real-world interest.

false_negative.name: False-Negative Trap
false_negative.risk: Rejecting a great idea because of an inadequate test setup (e.g. Dropbox AdWords failing because customers did not search for a new market).
false_negative.defense: Ensure testing mechanism aligns with market maturity. Recommend alternative channels.

local_maximum.name: Local Maximum Trap
local_maximum.risk: Optimizing around a mediocre local peak instead of exploring global mountains.
local_maximum.defense: Force the user to test at least three radically different business model directions in parallel before deep optimization.

exhausted_maximum.name: Exhausted Maximum Trap
exhausted_maximum.risk: Overlooking market size limits by assuming a small early adapter group represents the entire population.
exhausted_maximum.defense: Insist on validation parameters that prove scalability beyond initial test subjects.

wrong_data.name: Wrong Data Trap
wrong_data.risk: Dropping a great opportunity because you are looking at the wrong segment data.
wrong_data.defense: Re-run segment mapping using outliers as a bellwether for potential target adjustments.
